coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry dual-mode nano-sensor based on specific chelation for morin detection

Yang, Zhenhua
Hu, Qin
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorometry is an effective method for the quick and sensitive detection of the target objects by reducing the background interference. Carbon dots (Cdots), especially metal-doped carbon dots (M-Cdots), have been extensively developed as ratiometric fluorescence sensors and widely used in materials, chemistry, biomedicine, etc. Herein, we reported a simple and economical one-step hydrothermal method for the synthesis of gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-Cdots) by treating Gd(NO3)3.6H2O and 3-aminothiophenol. The Gd-Cdots afford good fluorescence properties, high T 1 relaxivity, and excellent biocompatibility, making them an intrinsical nano-sensor and dual-mode imaging reagent. Based on the unique chelation between Gd and morin, a ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor for the detection of morin was constructed with the linear range of 0.04–80.0 µM and the detection limit of 25.0 nM. Meanwhile, the color of the Gd-Cdots varied from colorless to deep-yellow, so a colorimetric nano-sensor was developed for morin detection with the linear ranges and the detection limits of 0.04–15.0 µM and 10.5 nM and 15.0–93.0 µM and 21.2 nM, respectively. Compared with the reported fluorescent or colorimetric sensors for the detection of morin, the constructed ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots owns higher sensitivity, lower detection limit, wider linear range, and less background interference. More importantly, the nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots was successfully applied to detect morin in human urine and living cells. Overall, the present study shows that the Gd-Cdots possess distinct optical and toxicological properties and are outstanding nanomaterials for the construction of nano-sensors and cellular imaging.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium-doped carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry dual-mode nano-sensor based on specific chelation for morin detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorometry is an effective method for the quick and sensitive detection of the target objects by reducing the background interference. Carbon dots (Cdots), especially metal-doped carbon dots (M-Cdots), have been extensively developed as ratiometric fluorescence sensors and widely used in materials, chemistry, biomedicine, etc. Herein, we reported a simple and economical one-step hydrothermal method for the synthesis of gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-Cdots) by treating Gd(NO3)3.6H2O and 3-aminothiophenol. The Gd-Cdots afford good fluorescence properties, high T 1 relaxivity, and excellent biocompatibility, making them an intrinsical nano-sensor and dual-mode imaging reagent. Based on the unique chelation between Gd and morin, a ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor for the detection of morin was constructed with the linear range of 0.04–80.0 µM and the detection limit of 25.0 nM. Meanwhile, the color of the Gd-Cdots varied from colorless to deep-yellow, so a colorimetric nano-sensor was developed for morin detection with the linear ranges and the detection limits of 0.04–15.0 µM and 10.5 nM and 15.0–93.0 µM and 21.2 nM, respectively. Compared with the reported fluorescent or colorimetric sensors for the detection of morin, the constructed ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots owns higher sensitivity, lower detection limit, wider linear range, and less background interference. More importantly, the nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots was successfully applied to detect morin in human urine and living cells. Overall, the present study shows that the Gd-Cdots possess distinct optical and toxicological properties and are outstanding nanomaterials for the construction of nano-sensors and cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence, an effective method for the quantitative determination of target substances, is realized by establishing the dual-signal readout mode between two luminous bodies [1,2]. The ratiometric detection method can greatly avoid the background interference and has an internal self-calibration function, which can overcome the limitations of single-wavelength detection method [3,4]. The ratiometric fluorescence sensors can improve the dynamic response range and establish an internal self-adjustment standard, which can effectively eliminate data distortion due to the interferences of many variables such as concentration, temperature, polarity, environmental pH value, and stability [5–7]. Therefore, constructing a late-model ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of the target object can significantly promote the tolerance of the sensor to the detection environment and improve the sensitivity and accuracy, which can broaden the application range of the sensor to the greatest extent.

Carbon dots (C-dots or CQDs or CDs or Cdots), an emerging star in luminescent materials, which have vast application potential in photoluminescence sensing, optoelectronic devices, and biomedical imaging, resulting from their high photostability, good biocompatibility, simple synthesis, and easy surface functionalization [8–11]. Heteroatom doping as a well-established strategy has been used to enhance the optical, electrical, and chemical properties of the Cdots [12–14]. In addition, single metal atom doped (such as iron [15], copper [16], or zinc [17]) Cdots have received sustaining attention in the carbon materials community, attributing to the physicochemical properties of the single metal-doped Cdots can be selectively regulated by controlling the reaction conditions and the type of metal atom, which can further explore the innovative applications of the single metal-doped Cdots. Among these metal dopants, gadolinium (Gd) shows excellent contrast effects in magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, so it is widely reported in the field of biological imaging [18]. Xu et al. demonstrated that the biocompatible CQD-stabilized Gd3+ hybrid nanoprobe (Gd-CQDs) with fluorescence and MR dual-response property was functionalized by arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) tripeptide, which can penetrate the blood-brain barrier and show high affinity in vivo U87 tumor-targeted imaging [19]. He et al. prepared two kinds of Gd-doped CDs (L-CD and C-CD) from different cationic polymers by one-pot hydrothermal method, which were applied in MR/FL imaging and gene delivery [20]. Only Fang et al. pointed that the fluorescence emission and MR signal of the synthesized Gd3+-doped C-dots can be changed after triggering by H+, OH-, or Cu2+, leading to dual-readout and multi-addressable logic systems [21]. But this work does not achieve the detection of the target objects. Based on this, we want to make full use of the advantages of Gd-doped carbon dots to construct nano-sensors and broaden the application range of Gd-doped carbon dots in the field of analysis and detection.

Morin, one of the most common flavonoids, is widely distributed in many vegetables, fruits, tea, moraceae plants, and herb medicine [22,23]. It exhibits various biological activities involving antibacterial, antiviral, anti-tumor, anti-radical, anti-inflammatory, and anti-allergic, which was successfully used in treating fever, preventing diabetes, reducing blood pressure, strengthening joints, resisting cancer, improving eyesight, and preventing cardiovascular diseases [24,25]. So, it is necessary to determine morin quickly, sensitively, and accurately. Diverse approaches have been established to determine morin, including electrochemical method [26,27], nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [28], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [29], fluorescence method [30], etc. Thereinto, fluorescence assay has attracted much attention due to its advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, easy operation, and free secondary pollution, so many fluorescence sensors based on the Cdots [23,30] and fluorescent polymers [31] have been constructed for the detection of morin. However, most of the fluorescence sensors for detecting morin in single-emission mode, which cannot avoid the interference of background and external factors. Meanwhile, the detection mechanism of morin is not clearly explained, limiting the further development of fluorescence sensors for detecting morin. In consequence, we want to develop a dual-mode nano-sensor for the detection of morin with ratiometric fluorescence to overcome background interference and with colorimetry to corroborate the determination results. By elucidating the mechanism of morin detection in detail, it provides a new idea for the construction of a nano-sensor to detect morin.

We herein report a novel and simple method for synthesizing the Gd-doped carbon dots (Gd-Cdots) by using Gd(NO3)3·6H2O and 3-aminothiophenol as the reaction precursors. Gd-Cdots exhibited remarkably photostability in long-term irradiation and high-concentration saline solution, which can be used as a ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor to detect morin ( Scheme 1). The color of the Gd-Cdots changed from colorless to deep-yellow during the detection of morin, making the Gd-Cdots can also be used as a colorimetric nano-sensor for assistive detection of morin (Scheme 1). Ultimately, the as-constructed dual-mode nano-sensor was applied for the detection of morin in human urine samples and live cells with satisfying results, which provides the possibility for clinical detection of morin based on the Cdots.

Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (99%), 3-aminothiophenol (97%), amino acids (99% histidine/His, 98% methionine/Met, 98% glutamic acid/Glu, 98% Glutathione/GSH, 97% arginine/Arg, 98% tryptophan/Trp, 98% isoleucine/Ile, 98% lysine/Lys, 98% leucine/Leu, 98% glutamine/Gln, 98% alanine/Ala, 99% proline/Pro, 98% threonine/Thr, 99% glycine/Gly, 98% phenylalanine/Phe, 98% tyrosine/Tyr, ≥ 95% cysteine/Cys, 98% serine/Ser, 98% valine/Val, and 98% aspartic acid/Asp), and drugs (AR vancomycin/VAN, 99% metronidazole/MTR, ≥ 98% oxytetracycline/OXY, AR azitromycin/AZM, AR penicillin/PEN, 98% cefotaxime/CTX, ≥ 98% ornidazole/ORN, 98% sulfamethoxazole/SMZ, 98% sulfadiazine/SDI, 95% kanamycin/KA, 99% thiamphenicol/THI, ≥ 90% morin, 95% cephradine/CED, ≥ 98% tinidazole/TIN, ≥ 98% clarithromycin/CLR, 98.5% ursolic acid/UA, 98% dopamine/DM, 95% lincomycin/LIN, AR gentamycin/GEN, ≥ 98% oleanolic acid/OA, ≥ 99% trimethoprim/TMP, and ≥ 98% amoxicillin/AMC) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). AR represents analytical reagent. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade or above and used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (≥ 18.25 MΩ·cm), obtaining from a Molecular purification system (Shanghai, China).

The apparatus models and relevant test conditions of transmission electron microscopic (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), elemental analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and fluorescence lifetime are consistent with our previously published paper [32]. TEM and AFM images were measured with a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) and a Veeco Nanoscope Quadrex AFM (Bruker AFM Sensors, Camarillo, CA, U.S.A.), respectively. XPS and elemental analysis were obtained by an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan) and an Elementar Analysen systeme vario EL CUBE elemental analyser (Hanau, Germany), respectively. FT-IR spectra were collected on a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). TGA was gotten from a TA Instruments Q50 thermal analyser (New Castle, DE, U.S.A.) under a stream of nitrogen (N2) gas with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were from a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, Worcestershire, U.K.). Fluorescence lifetimes were acquired on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, U.K.). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed on a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, U.K.) and FP-8300 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan), respectively. The determination of gadolinium (Gd) content was carried out on an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) (PerkinElmer, Avio 500, U.S.A).

0.1 g Gd(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.03 g 3-aminothiophenol were dissolved in 10 mL absolute ethanol by stirring and then heated at 180 °C for 12 h in the oven. The obtained brown-green suspension in the autoclave was orderly treated by evaporating ethanol, dissolving in ultrapure water, centrifuging (8000 rpm, 10 min), dialysing (100–500 Da, 12 h, changing the external solution every 3 h), and lyophilizing to obtain the solid powder of the Gd-Cdots. For comparison, under the same conditions, the Cdots were fabricated by treating individual 3-aminothiophenal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorometry for the detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of morin was performed at room temperature. Different concentrations of morin were added into the Gd-Cdots solution (0.1 mg·mL−1), and then the fluorescence spectra and intensities were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (λex at 364 nm). The experiment was repeated three times to calculate error bars. To further investigate the specificity of the nano-sensing system towards morin, different drugs were added into the Gd-Cdots solution as a substitute for morin under the same conditions (0.05 mM). Meanwhile, the anti-interference performance of the sensing system was also evaluated. First, different interferents (0.5 mM) were separately added into the Gd-Cdots aqueous solution (0.1 mg·mL−1) to measure the fluorescence intensities. Then, morin (0.05 mM) was introduced into the above-mixed solution, and the fluorescence emission spectra and intensities were accordingly recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetry for the detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: Morin with different concentrations was separately added into the Gd-Cdots solution (0.1 mg·mL−1). Then, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture solution were obtained in the range of 230–500 nm. To further assess the selectivity of the Gd-Cdots to morin, common amino acids, metal ions, and drugs with the final concentration of 0.05 mM were added into the Gd-Cdots solution (0.1 mg·mL−1) as a substitute for morin under the same conditions as described above. The anti-interference performance of the nano-sensing system was also evaluated. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded after introducing morin (0.05 mM) into the mixed solution (different interferents (0.5 mM) and the Gd-Cdots).

The human non-small-cell lung cancer cells (type A549) and rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cells (type PC 12) were cultured in DMEM medium containing 10% fetal calf serum (FBS), 100 U∙mL−1 penicillin, and 100 μg∙mL−1 streptomycin, which was incubated in an incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in air. The cells in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded in a 96-well culture plate (100 µL∙well−1) at a cell number of 8 × 104 mL−1, whereas the DMEM culture medium without cells was added into the outermost circle of the 96-well culture plate (blank groups). After 24 h of routine culture, the cells were divided into a control group and the drug-administered groups with six sets of duplicate holes in each group. The medium was changed with 100 µL of the prepared culture medium containing different concentrations of the Gd-Cdots, and then the cells were allowed to grow for another 24 h. That were not treated by the Gd-Cdots were taken as controls (control groups). After adding 20 µL of the MTT reagent (5.0 mg∙mL−1) into every well, the cells were further incubated for another 4 h. The supernatant was removed, and then 100 µL of DMSO was added into every well to dissolve the precipitate. The culture plate was shaken for 10 min, and then the optical density (OD) was measured at 570 nm. The survival rate of the cells was calculated by the following formula:

To explore the ability of the Gd-Cdots to recognize morin in the cells, A549 and PC 12 cells were cultured in a DMEM culture medium at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in air. The cells were inoculated in a laser confocal dish to incubate for about 24 h, and then the cells were treated by the Gd-Cdots (600 μg∙mL−1) to incubate for another 5 h. And then, the culture medium of the drug-administered group was removed and replaced by morin (56 μM) to incubate for another 1 h. At the same time, the control group did not perform any operation and was continuously incubated for 1 h. Finally, the cells were washed by Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS, pH = 7.4) for three times. The cells were kept in DPBS (500 µL) for cellular imaging using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (LSM880, Zeiss, Germany).

The Gd-Cdots were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of Gd(NO3)3.6H2O and 3-aminothiophenol at 180 °C for 12 h. As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM result shows that the Gd-Cdots are uniformly dispersed on the copper grid coated with ultra-thin carbon film, and the average particle size of the Gd-Cdots is 2.45 ± 0.91 nm with the distribution range of 0.4–5.0 nm. The representative HRTEM image of the Gd-Cdots in the inset of Fig. 1A demonstrates that the lattice spacing of the Gd-Cdots is ca. 0.19 nm. AFM image and height distribution of the Gd-Cdots were respectively depicted in Fig. 1B and C, displaying that the Gd-Cdots possess a narrow height distribution with a mean height of 3.2 nm. As depicted in Fig. S1, an average hydrodynamic size of the Gd-Cdots is ca. 3.62 nm, manifesting that the as-prepared Gd-Cdots are stable in ultrapure water without aggregation. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDXA) of the Gd-Cdots is displayed in Fig. 1D and E. It can be seen that the Gd-Cdots are composed of C, N, S, O, and Gd elements with the Gd content of 41.93 wt% (Fig. 1F), which is consistent with the results of ICP-OES (the content of Gd is 39.26%). These results demonstrate that the Gd-Cdots have spherical-like structure, good dispersion, and abundant Gd, which is a novel Cdots with excellent structure.

In order to investigate the chemical composition and surface functional groups of the prepared Gd-Cdots, the elemental analysis, XPS, and FTIR spectra were studied in detail. Elemental analysis in Table S1A summarizes the composition of the Gd-Cdots containing C (4.24 wt%), H (2.31 wt%), N (18.01 wt%), S (0.92 wt%), O (30.56 wt%), and Gd (calculated 43.96 wt%), which further corroborated the results of EDXA and ICP-OES. The Gd content in the Gd-Cdots obtained by three test methods is slightly different, which is attributed to the certain systematic errors of the different methods. However, the range of Gd content is about 39.0–44.0 wt%, indicating that the Gd content in the Gd-Cdots is relatively high [33]. The corresponding empirical formula for a Gd-Cdot is approximately C11H78N19SO64Gd11 (Table S1B). The XPS survey spectrum further indicates the presence of Gd(3d), Gd(4d), C(1s), N(1s), S(2p), and O(1s) ( Fig. 2A). The Gd(3d) spectra (Fig. 2B and C) show that the presence of two peaks were at 1219.50 eV and 1187.63 eV, respectively, corresponding to a spin-orbit coupled Gd3d 3/2 and Gd3d 5/2, which are the energy level of Gd3+ in Gd2O3 [19,33]. The high-resolution Gd(4d) spectrum (Fig. 2D) is dissociated with three typical binding energies at 149.03, 144.92, and 142.08 eV, being in agreement with Gd4d 3/2 Gd2O3, Gd4d 5/2 Gd(NO3)3, and Gd4d 5/2 Gd2O3, respectively [19,33,34]. The spectrum of C(1s) (Fig. S2A) reveals four main peaks at 286.42, 285.25, 284.74, and 284.28 eV, attributing to the presence of C-O, C-N, C-C/C-S/CC, and C-S, respectively [33,35]. The binding energy peaks of the N(1s) spectrum of the Gd-Cdots (Fig. 2E) at 407.24, 401.93, and 399.52 eV are corresponded to N-O/NO/N-H, graphitic-N, and pyrrolic-N, respectively [35]. For O(1s) spectrum, four peaks at 533.30, 532.80, 532.30, and 531.70 eV belong to Gd-C-O, C-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and C-N-O, respectively (Fig. S2B) [33,35,36]. Three peaks in the S(2p) spectrum (Fig. S2C) are assigned to S2p 3/2 S-O (172.26 eV), SO (168.87 eV), and S2p C-S (164.17 eV) [35,36]. Moreover, by calculating the area of each element in the XPS survey spectrum, the elemental composition ratio of the Gd-Cdots can be obtained, which confirmed that the content of Gd in the Gd-Cdots is about 42.17% within the range of 39.0–44.0 wt% (vide supra). As shown in Fig. 2F, FTIR was employed to further validate the chemical constitution and exterior structure of the Gd-Cdots. The Gd-Cdots exhibit an obvious vibration peak at around 3378 cm−1, which is ascribed to the O-H or N-H stretching vibrations [35,37]. The vibration peaks between 2509 and 2333 cm−1 are associated with the S-H stretching vibration [37,38]. The absorption peak at around 1635 cm−1 and the intense band at 1318 cm−1 is respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of CN and the stretching vibration of C-N, certificating the presence of the superficial hydrophilic groups of the Gd-Cdots [37]. In addition, the distinct absorption band at around 1479 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CC [37,39]. The slight peaks in the range of 1100–1200 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C-O, C-S, and C-C [40]. The distinct absorption band at around 1030 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of -SO3 [40]. All the results show that Gd was successfully doped into the Gd-Cdots, and the Gd-Cdots have abundant functional groups. Fig. S3 shows that the zeta potential (ζ) of the Gd-Cdots is 24.9 mV. In consequence, the abundant functional groups endow the Gd-Cdots with a positive charge, improving the hydrophilicity and stability of the Gd-Cdots in aqueous systems.

For the thermostability of the Gd-Cdots, the thermogravimetry curve of the Gd-Cdots was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min (Fig. S4). An initial weight loss of about 3.8% is observed around 100 °C for the Gd-Cdots, owing to the loss of adsorbed water. The weight loss of about 45.5% is found in a temperature range of 100–400 °C, inferring the entire carbonization and pyrolysis of the Gd-Cdots with the releasing of pyrogens (CO, CO2, NO, NO2, and SO2). Between 400 °C and 780 °C, the Gd-Cdots slowly lose the weight of 10.6%, mainly attributed to the degradation of the aromatic rings. The TGA curve tends to be smooth at the temperature of 780–900 °C. The residual weight of the Gd-Cdots is about 40.1%, which is mainly the inorganic Gd in the Gd-Cdots and consistent with the characterization results of Gd in the Gd-Cdots (vide supra). These results demonstrate that the Gd-Cdots have excellent thermostability.

To investigate the optical features of the Gd-Cdots, fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of the Gd-Cdots were recorded by fluorescence spectrofluorometer and UV/vis spectrophotometer, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the Gd-Cdots present a strong fluorescence emission peak at 485 nm when the excitation wavelength is 364 nm with bright cyan fluorescence under the illumination of a UV lamp (the inset in Fig. 3A). Compared Cdots synthesized in the same proportion without Gd showed a fluorescence emission peak at 525 nm when the excitation wavelength was 477 nm (Fig. S5). Moreover, the Gd-Cdots exhibit an excitation-dependent emission behavior in the excitation wavelengths of 360–440 nm (Fig. 3B), similar to other previously reported Cdots [15]. The luminescence color variation of the Gd-Cdots was elucidated with the CIE diagram (Fig. 3C), which is consistent with the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3B. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared Gd-Cdots (blue line in Fig. 3A) shows three peaks at around 262 nm, 300 nm, and 420 nm, which are ascribed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 bond, the n→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond, and n→π* transition of CO and CN bonds [41,42], respectively, associating with the surface states of the Gd-Cdots and being responsible for the luminescence of the Gd-Cdots. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized Gd-Cdots in the aqueous phase is calculated to be 0.65% (Fig. S6), using quinine sulfate as a reference. In comparison, the QY of the Cdots is 3.32% with fluorescein as a reference (Fig. S7). According to the FTIR spectrum of the C-dots (orange line in Fig. S8), the abundant amino group (3300–3500 cm−1) endows the good fluorescence property of the Cdots [43]. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of the Cdots, the peaks at 1318 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of C−N) [37] and 1479 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of CC) [37] were stronger, and the peaks at 1100–1200 cm−1 (the stretching vibrations of C−O, C−S, and C−C) [40] were weaker in the FTIR spectrum of the Gd-Cdots (blue line in Fig. S8). We infer that the doping of Gd can lead to the important changes in functional groups on the surface of the Cdots, which affects the fluorescence of the Cdots and leads to the fluorescence attenuation of the Cdots. Even so, the low QY of the Gd-Cdots does not affect its application and is conducive to the construction of sensitized fluorescence sensors (ratiometric type or fluorescence-open type). The longitudinal (r 1) relaxivity of the Gd-Cdots was investigated to demonstrate the successful doping of Gd in the Gd-Cdots. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the concentration-dependent has positive with T 1 contrast enhancement and the r 1 relaxivity of the Gd-Cdots was calculated to be 6.96 mM−1s−1 according to the Gd content of 39.26 wt% (ICP-OES), indicating that Gd has been successfully doped into the Gd-Cdots. Furthermore, the r
1 relaxivity was calculated according to the Gd content of 41.93 wt% (EDXA) and 43.96 wt% (elemental analysis) with the results of 6.52 mM−1s−1 (Fig. S9A) and 6.22 mM−1s−1 (Fig. S9B), respectively, demonstrating that the r
1 relaxivity of the Gd-Cdots is between 6.22 mM−1s−1 and 6.96 mM−1s−1. All results illustrate that the Gd-Cdots hold great potential as T 1-weighted MRI contrast agent in clinical application.

In order to broaden the application range of the Gd-Cdots, excellent property (vide supra) and good stability is necessary condition. The stability of the Gd-Cdots was estimated under xenon and UV lamp irradiation, high salt solution, amino acids, and metal ions. The effect of xenon and UV lamp irradiation on fluorescence behavior was examined. Under continuously irradiating by a xenon lamp (90 min, Fig. S10A) or UV lamp (300 min, Fig. S10B), the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots has observed no distinct change, suggesting that the Gd-Cdots exhibit high resistance to photo-bleaching. The fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots under different concentrations of KCl was evaluated, displaying that the Gd-Cdots reveal excellent stability under high ionic strength conditions (4.0 M, Fig. S10C), which is a significant advantage in environmental applications. Moreover, the fluorescence stability of the Gd-Cdots in different pHs solution was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S10D. The fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots is basically stable (75%~105% compared to the Gd-Cdots in ultrapure water) in a near-neutral pH range (5.0–8.0), indicating that the Gd-Cdots have great fluorescence stability in different pHs. The effects of various amino acids and metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots were studied. After various amino acids (His, Met, Glu, GSH, Arg, Trp, Ile, Lys, Leu, Gln, Ala, Pro, Thr, Gly, Phe, Tyr, Cys, Ser, Val, and Asp) and metal ions (Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Tl3+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Na+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Cr3+) were introduced into the Gd-Cdots solution, respectively, no obvious changes on the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots are observed, illustrating that the Gd-Cdots possess stabilized fluorescence property in the presence of different kinds of amino acids (Fig. S11A) and metal ions (Fig. S11B). All results indicate that the Gd-Cdots have excellent application potential in the field of environmental monitoring and bioimaging.

Because of the good fluorescence properties and excellent photostability of the Gd-Cdots, we further explored the application of the Gd-Cdots in the field of fluorescence sensors. We chose different kinds of drugs (VAN, MTR, OXY, AZM, PEN, CTX, ORN, SMZ, SDI, KAN, THI, morin, CED, TIN, CLR, UA, DM, LIN, GEN, OA, TMP, and AMC) for research objects; it can be seen in Fig. S12 that only morin can bring about the strong fluorescence enhancement of the Gd-Cdots, indicating that the Gd-Cdots have good selectivity to morin. Ulteriorly, it is possible to be affected by other potential interferents in the process of morin determination, so the anti-interference performance of the Gd-Cdots for the determination of morin should be explored. When a 10-fold concentration of interferences (0.5 mM) was existed in the Gd-Cdots, the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots remains largely unchanged (yellow bars in Fig. S13); while that is increased significantly after adding morin (0.05 mM) into the mixture system (blue bars in Fig. S13), demonstrating that the Gd-Cdots can be used to detect morin with good anti-interference. As shown in Fig. 4A, when different concentrations of morin (0–150 µM) were added into the Gd-Cdots, the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots at 490 nm is gradually increased while that at 415 nm is regularly decreased, so a ratiometric fluorescence “turn-on” nano-sensor can be constructed to detect morin. It is worth noting that the ratiometric and “turn-on” are important features for this nano-sensor, which can maximally eliminate environmental disturbance and efficiently improve the accuracy and reliability in the determination of morin. The CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4B) shows the fluorescence color of the Gd-Cdots changed from blue to cyan-green with the increase of morin, which is consistent with the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 4A. Fig. 4C depicts the titration process and the linear relationship between the Gd-Cdots and morin. In the range of 0.04–80.0 µM, the concentration of morin and the fluorescence ratio (I490 nm/I415 nm) exhibits good linear correlation with the linear equation of I490/I415 = 0.3663c(morin) + 1.5036 (R
2 = 0.9983). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) for morin can be reached to 25.0 nM, which is much lower than other existing detection methods for morin [23,26,29,44,45]. A comparison between the published analysis method for morin and the as-constructed ratiometric “turn-on” fluorescence nano-sensor is shown in Table 1, demonstrating that our method is comparable to other reported methods with wider linear range and lower detection limit.

The other important discovery is that the color of the Gd-Cdots visible to the naked eye was changed from almost colorless to deep yellow with the addition of morin into the Gd-Cdots (the first-row in Fig. 4D); meanwhile, the cyan-green fluorescence of the Gd-Cdots under UV light was increased gradually (the second-row in Fig. 4D), which is consistent with the results in Fig. 4A and B. Based on this, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Gd-Cdots were studied after the addition of various concentrations of morin (0–112.0 μM) into the Gd-Cdots, which is shown in Fig. 4E. In the titration process, the absorption peak at 280 nm was blue-shifted to 262 nm and enhanced significantly. In comparison, the other absorption peak at 420 nm was blue-shifted to 415 nm with an insignificant displacement and increased gradually, so 415 nm as characteristic absorption peak, was used to investigate the linear relationship between the morin concentration and the absorbance of 415 nm (Fig. 4F). A good linear relation at 0.04–15.0 μM and 15.0–93.0 μM can be found, with the linear regression equation and LOD of A/A0 = 0.5439c(morin) + 1.0627 (R
2 = 0.9918) and 10.5 nM and A/A0 = 0.2697c(morin) + 5.0105 (R
2 = 0.9975) and 21.2 nM, respectively. A and A0 is the absorbance of the Gd-Cdots at 415 nm in the presence and absence of morin, respectively. Therefore, a “turn-on” colorimetric nano-sensor was constructed to detect morin. In order to use the nano-sensor to detect morin in actual samples, the selectivity and anti-interference of the nano-sensor were investigated in detail. As depicted in Fig. S14, expect morin, all tested amino acids and drugs do not cause the enhancement of the absorbance at 415 nm, certifying the good selectivity of the colorimetric nano-sensor. When the 10-fold of the tested interferent (0.5 mM) was added into the Gd-Cdots, the absorbance at 415 nm was hardly enhanced (orange bars in Fig. S15). And then morin (0.05 mM) was continually added into the above-mixed solution. The absorbance at 415 nm was strengthened remarkably (green bars in Fig. S15), demonstrating that the colorimetric nano-sensor possesses good anti-interference for the detection of morin. All results illustrate that the as-constructed “turn-on” ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry nano-sensor can be used to detect morin with good selectivity, high anti-interference, and outstanding sensitivity and is expected to be applied in accurately detecting morin in real samples.

We infer that the fluorescence enhancement of the Gd-Cdots after adding morin is attributed to the chelation between Gd3+ of the Gd-Cdots and morin ( Fig. 5A) [46–48]. Morin has 3OH, 4CO, 5OH, and adjacent OH groups in the B ring, so it can coordinate with Gd3+ to form a coordination compound [46]. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were performed to further clarify the relevant inference. As shown in Fig. 5B, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of morin (blue line) has an obvious absorption peak at 359 nm, while a new absorption peak at 415 nm can be found with the addition of Gd3+ (4.8–48.0 µM), demonstrating that a new Gd-morin coordination compound was generated between morin and Gd3+. Similarly, the addition of morin (0.0–30.0 µM) makes a new absorption peak at 415 nm on the absorption spectra of the Gd-Cdots (Fig. 5C), which is consistent with the results in Fig. 5B. This result illustrates that the surface of the Gd-Cdots is rich in highly active Gd3+, providing abundant binding sites for morin. Furthermore, fluorescence lifetime was used to certify the formation of a new morin-Gd-Cdots coordination compound. The fluorescence decay curves of the Gd-Cdots without and with different concentrations of morin (10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 µM) were evaluated and is displayed in Fig. 5D. All decay curves were fitted by a three-exponential equation, and the relevant data are shown in Table S2. The average lifetime of the Gd-Cdots in the presence of morin (0, 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 µM) is gradually decreased from 2.24 ns to 0.465 ns, confirming the generation of the morin-Gd-Cdots coordination compounds with a shorter fluorescence lifetime than that of the Gd-Cdots. Furthermore, the fluorescence of morin at 490 nm can dramatically enhance in the presence of Gd3+; similarly, the fluorescence of the Gd-Cdots with morin at 490 nm was increased significantly (Fig. 5E), further elucidating the generation of the morin-Gd-Cdots coordination compound. We believe that the Gd-Cdots can chelate more morin with the increase of morin concentration. Therefore, different concentrations of morin correspond to different morin-Gd-Cdots coordination compounds. However, these coordination compounds have the common characteristics, a stronger fluorescence and a shorter lifetime compared to the Gd-Cdots. All the results demonstrate that the recognition effect of the Gd-Cdots on morin is mainly attributed to the specific chelation between Gd3+ on the surface of the Gd-Cdots and morin, which further determines the high selectivity of the as-constructed nano-sensor for morin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reliability for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition experiment by spiking different concentrations of morin in human urine was used to prove the practicability of the ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry dual-mode nano-sensor. Before analysis, the urine samples were filtered through a size of 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove the particle suspension, and then they were stored at 4 °C for further analysis. As summarized in Table 2, the desirable recoveries between 97.16% and 102.13% are obtained with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.62–5.26% (n = 6), meaning that the ratiometric fluorometric nano-sensor possesses potential reliability for the detection of morin in actual samples. Table 3 shows that the quantitative spike recoveries are determined to be 97.63–105.01% in human urine samples, and the RSDs are less than 6.15% (n = 6), approving the reliability and feasibility of the developed colorimetric nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots for detecting morin in real samples. These results illustrate that the nano-sensor has good selectivity, high anti-interference performance, and excellent repeatability and is available for the detection of morin in complex biological samples. Two detection modes (ratiometric fluorometry and colorimetry) of this nano-sensor have a highly coincident linear range (vide supra). The two modes can be used to mutually verify the detection results, improving the accuracy of the detection results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and application of the Gd-Cdots in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The Gd-Cdots show excellent and stable fluorescence performance, possessing great potential application in biological imaging. Thus, the cytotoxicity of the Gd-Cdots should be meticulously pondered. MTT assay was applied to assess the viability of human non-small-cell lung cancer cell (type A549) and rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cell (type PC 12) in the Gd-Cdots. Two cells were treated with the different concentrations of the Gd-Cdots (0, 150, 300, 450, 600, and 700 μg∙mL−1) for 48 h, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S16, the viability of A549 and PC 12 cells is still higher than 99% and 98% after they were incubated with a high concentration of the Gd-Cdots (700 μg∙mL−1) for 48 h, respectively, demonstrating the naturally low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility of the Gd-Cdots.

Because of the excellent biocompatibility of the Gd-Cdots, we further explored the potential of the as-constructed nano-sensor for the detection of morin in PC 12 and A549 cells. For the purpose of capturing the response of the Gd-Cdots to morin in live cells under a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM), the cells were respectively incubated with the Gd-Cdots without and with morin. It can be seen from the first-row and the third-row in Fig. 6, the cells treating by the Gd-Cdots emit weak blue fluorescence in the cytoplasm but rarely in the nucleus, confirming that the Gd-Cdots can penetrate the cell membrane and translocate into the cell by endocytosis. Bright blue fluorescence can be observed in cells (the second-row and the fourth-row in Fig. 6), where the cells were orderly incubated by the Gd-Cdots and morin. The calculation results of the fluorescence intensities are shown in the fourth column in Fig. 6, showing that the fluorescence intensity of the cells treating by the Gd-Cdots and morin is twice that of the cells treating by the Gd-Cdots. Morin can enhance the fluorescence of the Gd-Cdots in living cells, which is consistent with the results in an aqueous solution (vide supra). These results justify sufficiently that the Gd-Cdots can serve as a “turn-on” nano-sensor for real-time monitoring of morin in live cells.

In summary, we provide a simple and rapid preparation method by treating 3-aminothiophenol and Gd(NO3).6H2O, serving as carbon and nitrogen sources and Gd dopant, respectively. The as-prepared Gd-Cdots possess excellent optical properties, high T 1 relaxivity, and good biocompatibility, showing unlimited possibilities for application in analytical detection and cell imaging. Given the specific chelation behavior between morin and Gd3+, a new Gd-morin coordination compound with high fluorescence was generated. Gd-Cdots contain large amounts of Gd, so it can specifically chelate with morin to generate the morin-Gd-Cdots coordination compound and enhance the fluorescence of the Gd-Cdots. Based on this, a ratiometric “turn-on” fluorometric nano-sensor based on the Gd-Cdots was constructed to detect morin. Meanwhile, the Gd-Cdots can be served as a colorimetric nano-sensor to realize morin detection. The nano-sensor possesses low background interference, good selectivity, and high sensitivity, which was applied to detect morin in human urine samples and live cells with satisfying results, confirming the practicability of the nano-sensor. This work provides a novel idea for designing the specific nano-sensors based on the Cdots and broadens the application range of metal-doped Cdots.

Quantum yield measurements of the Gd-Cdots and the C-dots; DLS result, elemental analysis, C(1s), O(1s), S(2p), zeta potential, and TGA curve of the Gd-Cdots; fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the C-dots; plots of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance of quinine sulfate, Gd-Cdots, fluorescein, and C-dots; FTIR spectra of the Cdots and the Gd-Cdots; the r
1 relaxivity of the Gd-Cdots with the Gd content of 41.93 wt% and 43.96 wt%; effect of Xe lamp irradiation, UV irradiation, ionic strength, and pH on the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots; comparison of the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots in the presence of amino acids, metal ions, or drugs; comparison of the fluorescence intensity of the Gd-Cdots in the presence of different amino acids and drugs without and with morin; comparison of the absorbance at 415 nm of the Gd-Cdots in the presence of different amino acids and drugs; comparison of the absorbance at 415 nm of the Gd-Cdots in the presence of different amino acids and drugs without and with morin; triple-exponential fitting of the decay curves of the Gd-Cdots in the absence and presence of different concentrations of morin; cell viability test of the Gd-Cdots on A549 and PC 12 cells. (PDF).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huiping Wang received her BSc degree in Environmental Science in 2017 from Bohai University. She is currently pursuing her Doctoral degree at Shanxi University and major in Environmental Science.

Huaizhong Xing received his MSc degree in Physical Education in 2005 from Shanxi Normal University. He is currently an intermediate engineer in Integrated Center for Inspection and Testing of Changzhi City.
Wei Liu received his BSc degree in Physics in 1994 from Shanxi University. He is currently a senior engineer in Integrated Center for Inspection and Testing of Changzhi City.
Yumin Hao received his BSc degree in Environmental Engineering in 2019 from Shanxi University. He is currently pursuing his Master degree at Shanxi University and major in Environmental Science.

Li Zhang received her BSc degree in Environmental Ecological Engineering in 2018 from Shanxi University. She is currently pursuing her Doctoral degree at Shanxi University and major in Environmental Science.

Zhenhua Yang is an associate professor at Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University. He received his PhD degree in environmental science in 2013 from Shanxi University. He has published some research articles in journals such as Food and Chemical Toxicology, Environmental Science and Pollution Research, ACS Chemical Biology, etc.

Qin Hu is an associate professor at College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University. She graduated with a PhD degree in 2015 from Hong Kong Baptist University, Hong Kong. She has authored more than twenty research articles, including Analytica Chimica Acta, Food Chemistry, RSC Advances, Journal of Chromatography A, Electrophoresis, Journal of Nanomaterials, etc.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of analytical chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her BSc degree in chemistry and MSc degree in analytical chemistry in 1986 and 1989 from Shanxi University, respectively, and PhD degree in 1998 from South China University of Technology. She has published over 200 papers and her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, super-molecular chemistry, and photochemistry.

Chuan Dong is a professor of analytical chemistry at Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University. He received his BSc degree in chemistry and MSc degree in analytical chemistry in 1984 and 1990, respectively, and PhD degree in 2002 from Shanxi University. He has extensively published in journals such as Chemistry of Materials, Nanoscale, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta, etc. His current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Xiaojuan Gong is currently a professor at Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University. She received her BSc degree and PhD degree in environmental science in 2011 and 2016 from Shanxi University, respectively. She has published more than forty research articles extensively in journals such as Chemistry of Materials, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Analytica Chimica Acta, Talanta, Microchimica Acta, etc. Her research interests are in the areas of carbon nanoparticles, chemical and biological sensor, environmental analysis, and diagnosis and therapy of cancer.
